coredata.dc:title: Sulfur doping pyrene-based carbon dots with multicolor room temperature phosphorescence for information encryption
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zelin
Liu, Fengshan
Xu, Yang
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: The growing demands for multiple-level optical information encryption require the development of novel room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with long afterglow performance and multi-color phosphorescence. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new generation of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, have received widespread attention. Here, a series of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) with different sulfur doping regulation are proposed as emitters to fabricate RTP materials by embedding boric acid (BA) matrix. With different sulfur doping amount, S-CDs exhibit green, yellow-green and orange RTP, respectively. The RTP spectrum can be split into three emission centers with different intensities. Thus, the multi-color RTP emission is ascribed to varied surface state of S-CDs involving the phosphorescence center intensity changes, which results from sulfur doping. The RTP lifetimes of these S-CDs are calculated to be 579.28, 540.87, and 288.89 ms, which is measured for S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA, respectively. In addition, the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) is 27.93 %, 10.62 % and 13.58 %, respectively. The encryption test is performed utilizing these multi-color RTP S-CDs, demonstrating that these S-CDs have good RTP performance and are suitable for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable room temperature phosphorescence
Sulfur doping
Information encryption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support by the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (No. B2021202056 ) is acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The HR-TEM images of S-CDs-G, b) The particle size distributions of S-CDs-G.
The FT-IR spectra of a) S-CDs-G, b) S-CDs-Y and c) S-CDs-O, the XPS survey spectra of d) S-CDs-G, e) S-CDs-Y and f) S-CDs-O.
The High-resolution XPS spectra of a) C 1s, b) N1s, c) O1s and d) S2p of S-CDs-G.
The digital photographs of S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA before and after turning off 365 nm UV light.

The fluorescence emission spectra of a) S-CDs-G@BA, b) S-CDs-Y@BA and c) S-CDs-O@BA, the phosphorescence emission spectra of d) S-CDs-G@BA, e) S-CDs-Y@BA and f) S-CDs-O@BA under excitation at 365 nm fitted by Gaussian function, the RTP lifetime of g) S-CDs-G@BA, h) S-CDs-Y@BA and i) S-CDs-O@BA at the optimal emission wavelength.
The FT-IR spectra of a) S-CDs-G@BA, b) S-CDs-Y@BA and c) S-CDs-O@BA, d) The XRD patterns of S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA.
The schematic illustration of the generation of RTP (Ex: Excite, FL: Fluorescence, Vib.: Vibration).
a) Photographs of the “HEBUT” pattern made from S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA. UV light: 365 nm. b) Morse password pattern made from S-CDs-G@BA for information encryption (UV light: 365 nm).
The schematic illustration of S-CDs@BA synthesis process and RTP performances.

head.ce:title: Sulfur doping pyrene-based carbon dots with multicolor room temperature phosphorescence for information encryption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin, 300401, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Xiping Road No. 5340, Beichen District, Tianjin, 300401, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing demands for multiple-level optical information encryption require the development of novel room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials with long afterglow performance and multi-color phosphorescence. Carbon dots (CDs), as a new generation of carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, have received widespread attention. Here, a series of sulfur-doped carbon dots (S-CDs) with different sulfur doping regulation are proposed as emitters to fabricate RTP materials by embedding boric acid (BA) matrix. With different sulfur doping amount, S-CDs exhibit green, yellow-green and orange RTP, respectively. The RTP spectrum can be split into three emission centers with different intensities. Thus, the multi-color RTP emission is ascribed to varied surface state of S-CDs involving the phosphorescence center intensity changes, which results from sulfur doping. The RTP lifetimes of these S-CDs are calculated to be 579.28, 540.87, and 288.89 ms, which is measured for S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA, respectively. In addition, the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) is 27.93 %, 10.62 % and 13.58 %, respectively. The encryption test is performed utilizing these multi-color RTP S-CDs, demonstrating that these S-CDs have good RTP performance and are suitable for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have inspired extensive attention thanks to their long lifetime emission [1,2], and applied in the fields of anti-counterfeiting [3–5], information encryption [6,7], biological imaging [8,9], light emitting diode [10,11] and other aspects. In particular, with the increasingly rampant counterfeiting and shoddy products, owing to their high concealment, convenient use, and easy identification [12–14], multi-color RTP materials have become prominent in the fields of advanced anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and so on [15–18]. However, the phosphorescence red shift is still a challenge in recent stage [19–22], which severely limits the development of long wavelength and multi-color RTP materials. Therefore, exploring a new generation of materials with tunable RTP emission is highly desirable.

Recently, as a new type of luminescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much attention of scientists because of low toxicity, low cost, and high stability [23]. Due to their plentiful surface functional groups, CDs have become vital for fostering peculiar RTP materials [24] and several strategies have been proposed, including the crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect of CDs and embedding CDs in host matrix [25]. In particular, matrix-assisted method has been a well-known strategy in recent years, which can effectively suppress the nonradiative decay of the triplet exciton [26]. These matrices include polyvinyl alcohol [27], urea [28], silicon dioxide [29], boric acid [30] and inorganic salts [31], etc. For example, Xu. and coworkers [32] prepared five kinds CDs from different precursors by hydrothermal method, and then embedded them in metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) to construct five RTP materials with various emission colors. Liang et al. [33] reported that three different CDs were embedded into hydroxy fluorides to construct RTP materials with green, yellow and orange phosphorescence. From this point of view, multiple precursors participated in the preparation of multi-color RTP materials [34]. In this case, Ding et al. [35] successfully prepared full-color RTP materials by adjusting the proportion of CDs in the matrix and the reaction temperature, which was attributed to the increase of the degree of oxidation of CDs. Julin Joseph and coworkers [36] realized the red shift of RTP emission wavelength by doping heteroatoms. Although some relevant works have been reported, there is scarce research on the preparation of multi-color RTP materials using hydrophobic precursors such as pyrene.

Herein, we proposed sulfur-doping method to synthesize multi-color RTP materials by changing the reaction ratio of sodium sulfide (Na2S) and nitrated pyrene. Firstly, three kinds sulfur-doped CDs (S-CDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method at different Na2S addition amount. Then, the S-CDs were embedded in boric acid (BA) matrix to prepare S-CDs@BA RTP materials. The schematic diagram was shown in Scheme 1 . With the amount of Na2S reaction increased, both the sulfur doping amount and oxidation degree on the surface of S-CDs were increased. After combining with BA matrix, the phosphorescence emission of these composites changed from green to orange, which was due to the change of surface state of S-CDs. The feasible adjustment process and distinct phosphorescence characteristics demonstrate these S-CDs@BA materials provide a new route for adjusting RTP properties.

Pyrene (purity: 97 %) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Nitric acid (HNO3, purity: 65 %) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Boric acid (BA, purity: 99.5 %) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, purity: AR) was purchased from Tianjin wind boat chemical reagent Technology Co., Ltd.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the S-CDs were collected by a JEM 2100F (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy of these S-CDs were acquired by a Bruker Vector 22 spectrophotometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data of S-CDs were collected by a Thermo SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of S-CDs@BA composite materials were measured at room temperature with a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FS920P). The phosphorescence lifetime was performed by the fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FS920P). The UV–visible spectra of S-CDs@BA were performed by an Agilent Carry 100 UV–vis spectrometer. The X-ray diffractometer (XRD) data of S-CDs@BA composite materials were surveyed by MiniFlex600 (Neo-Confucianism Co., Ltd., Japan).

Briefly, the S-CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal reactions using nitrated pyrene as the sole precursor. Firstly, according to the previously reported method [37], nitrated pyrene was prepared by stirring and refluxing 1 g pyrene and 80 mL 65 % wt. HNO3 at 80 °C for 12 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the mixture was washed and filtered with a large amount of deionized water. Finally, the sample was obtained after drying at 70 °C.

Then, 100 mg nitrated pyrene and x mg (x = 64, 128, 192) of Na2S·9H2O were dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water. The above mixture was subjected to ultrasonic reaction for 1 h, and then transferred to a 50 mL hydrothermal synthesis reactor and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid powers were collected following the treatment of centrifugation, filtration dialysis and freeze dry. Dispersed the above CDs in water for further use and characterization, and denoted them as S-CDs-G, S-CDs-Y and S-CDs-O, respectively.

Typically, the 0.5 mL, 1 mL and 2 mL S-CDs and 3 g BA were dissolved in a beaker filled with 40 mL water, and dissolved by ultrasonic. The beaker was covered with foil to avoid rapid evaporation of the solution. Then, the composites powder was obtained by heating at 180 °C for 5 h, and grinding for further use. In order to study the mechanism of phosphorescence, CDs without sulfur doping (CDs-NP) and CDs-NP@BA were prepared, which was similar with S-CDs preparation process but no Na2S addition.

The S-CDs derived from nitrated pyrene as carbon source were prepared by adding different amount of Na2S (Scheme 1). The nitrated pyrene was obtained via a nitration process, and its FT-IR spectra exhibited the peaks at 1352 and 1523 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C─NO2 and Ar─NO2 [38], respectively, as shown in Fig. S1. It indicated that the pyrene had been nitrated successfully, similar with other previously work [37]. Hydrothermal reaction between nitrated pyrene and Na2S was performed to construct S-CDs. Different phosphorescence signals from green to orange could be observed in the series of composites, when the three kinds S-CDs were embedded into BA matrix, respectively. Therefore, we speculated that the doping of S was a vital factor to achieve multi-color RTP of pyrene-based CDs. According to RTP results, these three CDs were denoted as S-CDs-G, S-CDs-Y, and S-CDs-O, respectively.

The HR-TEM images demonstrated the S-CDs-G was nearly quasi spherical and had average particle sizes of 4.75 nm (Fig. 1 a–b), but no obvious lattice space was observed in the S-CDs-G sample. To understand the composition of S-CDs, further characterization was executed. The functional groups on the surface of these S-CDs could be identified by analyzing the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a–c). The surface functional groups of the three S-CDs had similar structures, and the stretching vibration peaks at about 3150–3580, 1625–1568, 1423, 1344, and 1150–1040 cm−1 were assigned to ─OH/N─H, C═N/C═O/C═C, CO─NH2 [39], C─NO2, and S─O/C─N/C─O [40,41], respectively. The location of 935 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C─S/C═S, which confirmed that the S-CDs was functionalized by C─SOx related electron-withdrawing group [42]. As a comparison, Fig. S2 showed Ar─H (3089 cm−1), C═C (1591 cm−1), Ar─NO2 (1521 cm−1), C─NO2 (1350 cm−1) and C─N (1193 cm−1) in CDs-NP, but the peaks ascribed to N─H, C─S and C═S were absent. The FT-IR spectrum results exhibited that the three S-CDs contained many functional groups that contributed to the appearance of phosphorescence, such as C─N and C─S bonds [43].

The XPS spectra were recorded for further analysis. Fig. 2d–f showed the basic element composition of the three S-CDs: C 1s (284.98 eV), N 1s (399.84 eV), O 1s (531.97 eV), S 2s (232.97 eV) and S 2p (168.97 eV). Due to the addition of a large amount of Na2S during the synthesis process and the presence of electrostatic adsorption between S-CDs and Na ions, the trace Na signal could be observed in the XPS spectra [44]. The element atomic contents of the three S-CDs could be obtained from Table S1. From this, it could be concluded that the N atomic contents were 8.25 %, 2.49 % and 2.21 %, the O atomic contents were 29.81 %, 33.61 % and 37.65 % and the S atomic contents were 2.39 %, 4.64 % and 5.43 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p of S-CDs-G were shown in Fig. 3 a–d. In Fig. 3a, the C 1s spectra of the S-CDs-G exhibited three peaks at about 284.6, 285.86, and 288.13 eV, which implied the existence of C─C/C═C, C─N/C─O/C─S, and C═O bonds, respectively. The XPS N 1s spectra (Fig. 3b) of the S-CDs-G revealed three peaks at about 399.57, 401.04 and 406.54 eV, which were attributed to C═N─C, N─H and N─O [38] groups, respectively. And, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) of the S-CDs-G had two peaks at about 531.05 and 532.05 eV due to the presence of C═O and C─O/N─O/S─O groups [34,41]. Additionally, the high-resolution S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3d) of the S-CDs-G revealed two peaks at about 164.05 and 168.45 eV, which were attributed to C─S and C─SOx (x = 2, 3, 4) bonds [36,42].

Figs. S3 and S4 showed the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p of S-CDs-Y and S-CDs-O, respectively. The XPS spectrum results proved that the three S-CDs had similar chemical structures. The difference was that with the increase of Na2S addition, the N atomic contents decreased from 8.25 % to 2.21 %, the oxidation degree of S-CDs structure gradually increased from 20.75 % to 48.97 % (the relative amount of C═O) and S doping content also increased from 2.39 % to 5.43 % in Tables S1 and S2 [35,45]. Therefore, it could be inferred that a large amount of Na2S reacted with N─O and promoted the participation of O and S in the carbonization process of nitrated pyrene, resulting in an increase in O and S atomic content and a decrease in N atomic content. Due to the presence of C═O and C─S with strong spin-orbit coupling ability, the triplet states were effectively populated, promoting the generation of RTP [33].

Fig. 5a–c showed the fluorescence emission spectra of three composites upon 365 nm UV excitation, indicated that the optimal emission wavelengths of S-CDs-G@BA and S-CDs-Y@BA composites were centered at 478 nm and 480 nm, respectively. However, a new emission peak of the S-CDs-O@BA appeared at 534 nm. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 5d–f, the phosphorescence emission peaks maxima of the three composites were 496, 552 and 598 nm, respectively. It could be observed that there were multiple peaks in the phosphorescence emission spectrum. Therefore, it was inferred that there might be multiple luminescent centers. To further demonstrate the phenomenon, the excitation-emission RTP mappings of S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA composites were measured. Fig. S5 exhibited that there were different emissions under different excitations, and the emission centers of the three composites had shifted to a certain extent, indicating the existence of multiple luminescent centers. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of S-CDs@BA was measured. In Fig. S6, the absorption peaks of S-CDs-G@BA at 225 and 268 nm were attributed to π–π* transition of C═C bond, and multiple absorption peaks at 316–375 nm were attributed to n–π* transition of C═O/C─S bond [46]. The absorption peaks of S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA were similar to S-CDs-G@BA. The absorption spectrum at 316–375 nm was within the excitation range of 365 nm, so the above-mentioned RTP emission might be originated from the C═O/C─S bonds.

The phosphorescence lifetime of the three S-CDs@BA composites at the optimal emission wavelength under 365 nm excitation were measured and shown in Fig. 5g–i and Table S3. The results could be obtained by double exponential fitting with the following formula [47]:
τ
a v g
=
∑
α i
τ i 2
∑
α i
τ i

The lifetimes were therefore calculated to be 579.28, 540.87, and 288.89 ms, which was measured at 496, 552 and 598 nm for S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA, respectively. And, as shown in Fig. S7, the phosphorescence quantum yield (PQY) of the three composites were 27.93 %, 10.62 % and 13.58 %, demonstrating excellent phosphorescence performance.

To further explore the phenomenon of multiple emission peaks, the emission peaks could be fitted as three phosphorescence emission centers by Gauss function [48], as shown in Fig. 5d–f and Table S4. The fitting data showed that the three phosphorescence emission centers of the three composites had different emission intensities, and the emission centers with the highest emission intensities were 493 nm, 531 nm and 599 nm, which might be the reason for the different phosphorescence colors [49].

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded to obtain the RTP generation mechanism of S-CDs@BA (Fig. 6 a–c). Compared with precursor, the S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA composites appeared the characteristic absorption band at about 3250, 1454, 1195 and 910 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibration of O─H, B─O, C─O─B and C─B, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6d, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA exhibited characteristic B2O3 peaks at about 14.9°, 28.2° and 40.4°, which corresponded to the PDF#06–0297 of B2O3. The data of FT-IR spectrum and XRD results of the S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA composites were consistent with the previous researches [35,50,51]. Combined with these experiences, it can be shown that the three S-CDs are successfully dispersed in the matrix mainly composed of B2O3, which can stabilize the triple excited state of S-CDs and suppress the non-radiative transition [52].

It was worth noting that the material synthesized by hydrothermal method using only BA exhibited very weak green RTP emission under 365 nm excitation, which indicated the RTP was attributed to S-CDs rather than BA (Fig. S8). To further study the RTP characteristics, a series of controlled experiments were designed to clarify the mechanism. Above all, by comparing the CDs-NP with the three S-CDs, we found that new functional groups, such as C═O/C─O, CO─NH2, S─O and C═S/C─S, appeared in the S-CDs after hydrothermal treatment with Na2S, but the blank CDs-NP did not have these functional groups (Fig. S2). As shown in Fig. S9, the CDs-NP@BA composite only showed weak RTP emission under 365 nm excitation. According to previous research, the phosphorescence process is generated by photoexcited particles going through inter-system crossing (ISC) from the lowest singlet state (S1) to the lowest triplet state (T1), and then radiating relaxation to its ground state (S0) [53]. However, the C═O and C─S bonds of S-CDs exhibit strong spin-orbit coupling, which can effectively populate the triplet states via an ISC process, which is conducive to phosphorescence emission. In addition, due to the electrostatic adsorption of S-CDs with Na ions, the influence of Na ions on the RTP was further investigated. Taking S-CDs-G as an example, a certain amount of NaNO3 were added to the preparation process of S-CDs-G@BA, and the final product was named S-CDs-G + NaNO3@BA. The phosphorescence emission spectra under 365 nm UV excitation exhibited that a large amount of Na ions had no significant effect on the phosphorescence emission peaks (Fig. S10). It can be concluded that the trace Na ions absorbed by S-CDs not affect the RTP performance.

To further clarify the multi-color RTP emission mechanism, different ratios of S-CDs (S-CDs-G, S-CDs-Y and S-CDs-O) and BA were used to prepare S-CDsx@BA (x symbols the volume of added S-CDs). As shown in Figs. S11–13, the phosphorescence emission peak wavelength of the S-CDs@BA composites covered the range of blue-green light to orange light which was from 450 to 600 nm. Figs. S11a–d showed that the main phosphorescence emission center of the S-CDs-G0.5@BA, S-CDs-G1@BA (S-CDs-G@BA) and S-CDs-G2@BA was around 493 nm, and there were two weak shoulder peaks at about 548 and 596 nm, which were slightly enhanced with the increase of S-CDs content, but the overall emission color was mainly 493 nm. Similarly, in Fig. S12a, we observed that S-CDs-Y0.5@BA (S-CDs-Y@BA) displayed phosphorescence emission centered at 477, 531 and 605 nm, and the emission center at 531 nm occupied a dominant position in its phosphorescence emission spectrum, so it mainly displayed yellow-green emission. With the increase of S-CDs-Y content, the intensity of 605 nm emission center gradually increased, and the emission center of 605 nm acted a major role in the S-CDs-Y1@BA, so it mainly exhibited orange emission (Fig. S12b). It indicated that the S-CDs-Y was a “transition state CDs”. The orange emission center was enhanced to produce RTP color changes with the increase of the S-CDs-Y content in the BA matrix [54]. In Figs. S13a–c, it could also be observed that as the S-CDs-O content increased, the intensity of the long wavelength emission center also increased. However, it might be due to a change in the surface state of S-CDs, which increased the intensity of the long wavelength emission center, leading to the occurrence of this phenomenon.

Combining with the XPS data of S-CDs on Table S1 and Fig. 2g–h, we found that with the increase of Na2S addition, the three S-CDs had rich functional groups, and the sulfur element doping amount and oxidation degree of S-CDs gradually increased. Referring to the previous research work involving multiple phosphorescent emission centers [35,36,48], the above research results showed that the high degree of CDs oxidation was beneficial for enhancing the intensity of yellow and orange phosphorescent emission centers. Just like the phosphorescence emission spectra fitted by the Gaussian function (Fig. 5d–f), the phosphorescence emission color changed from green to orange as the optimal emission center moved from 493 to 531 and 599 nm, which just verified the generation of multi-color phosphorescence of this composite.

In a word, the reasons for multi-color RTP emission can be attributed to the following two points: (i) the C═O and C─S bonds of S-CDs exhibit strong spin-orbit coupling, and the triplet state of photo-excited particles can be stabilized through the role of the matrix, which promotes the generation of phosphorescence, as shown in Fig. 7 ; (ii) with the increase of S-CDs in oxidation degree (the relative amount of C═O) from 20.75 % to 48.97 % and sulfur content from 2.39 % to 5.43 %, the intensity of long wavelength emission center of phosphorescence is enhanced, consequently producing different RTP emission colors.

Because of different RTP capabilities of the three S-CDs composites, it seems that information encryption may be a prospective application. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the S-CDs-G@BA, S-CDs-Y@BA and S-CDs-O@BA were used to simulate a simple security anti-counterfeiting graphic. When the UV light was turned off, the pattern exhibited green “HE”, yellow-green “B” and orange “UT”, forming a combination pattern of different colors, which indicated that S-CDs can be applied to multi-color conversion anti-counterfeiting. Meantime, in Fig. 8b, a Morse code for information encryption was demonstrated by using S-CDs-G@BA and thermal annealed BA. Under the illumination of daylight and UV light, the Morse code pattern appeared as follows “− − −, · · − ·, · · − ·“, which meant “OFF”. Then, after turning off the UV lamp, the pattern showed the green pattern “− − −, − ·“, which described the meaning of “ON”. In the way, we can give special meaning to the information letters through the password separately, which is used for advanced information encryption. Thus, these RTP materials have enormous development potential in multi-color anti-counterfeiting pattern and information encryption.

In summary, we proposed a general strategy for preparing multi-color RTP S-CDs composites. The S-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal method using nitrated pyrene and sodium sulfide. By changing the reaction amount of sodium sulfide, the sulfur doping amount and oxidation degree of S-CDs were adjusted. Hereby, three different phosphorescent emission colors composites were successfully prepared by combining S-CDs with boric acid matrix. The materials exhibited a high PQY (27.93 %, 10.62 % and 13.58 %) value when excited at 365 nm. Experimental results proved that the sulfur doping and oxidation degree played an important role in phosphorescent emission regulation. Furthermore, these composites with excellent multi-color phosphorescence performance were promising in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting technology. These results can provide a simple strategy for the construction of multi-color, promoting their applications in information security and protection.

Although some works on multi-color RTP CDs have been reported, pyrene-based CDs for RTP study is still in its nascent stages. To explore the RTP of pyrene-based CDs is still desired to extend new realms, such as microplastics tracing, because of the fluorescence staining activity of pyrene-based CDs [38]. It will provide a new research route to endow pyrene-based CDs with applications in the field of RTP model probes.

Zelin Zhang: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Fengshan Liu: Formal analysis, Validation. Yang Xu: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Huanrong Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the Hebei Natural Science Foundation (No. B2021202056) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2024.111998.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultralong room temperature phosphorescence and reversible mechanochromic luminescence in ionic crystals with structural isomerism
Chem Eng J

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Nie F, Zhou B, Yan DP. Ultralong room temperature phosphorescence and reversible mechanochromic luminescence in ionic crystals with structural isomerism. Chem Eng J. 2023;453:139806.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Defects and structural limitation-induced carbon dots-silica hybrid materials with ultralong room temperature phosphorescence
J Phys Chem Lett

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chao TY, Wang JJ, Dong XZ, Ren JK, Zhang HL, Song R, et al. Defects and Structural Limitation-Induced Carbon Dots-Silica Hybrid Materials with Ultralong Room Temperature Phosphorescence. J Phys Chem Lett. 2022;13(41):9558-9563.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications
Adv Mater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang K, Wang YH, Cai CZ, Lin HW. Conversion of Carbon Dots from Fluorescence to Ultralong Room-Temperature Phosphorescence by Heating for Security Applications. Adv Mater. 2018;30(26):1800783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room-temperature phosphorescent fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots: information encryption and anti-counterfeiting
